name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَٱلنَّجْمِ إِذَا هَوَىٰ (١)
```

```english
By the star when it sets: (1)
```

```arabic
مَا ضَلَّ صَاحِبُكُمْ وَمَا غَوَىٰ (٢)
```

```english
your companion has neither gone astray, nor amiss. (2)
```

```arabic
وَمَا يَنطِقُ عَنِ ٱلْهَوَىٰٓ (٣)
```

```english
Nor does he speak out of [his own] desire: (3)
```

```arabic
إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا وَحْىٌ يُوحَىٰ (٤)
```

```english
it is just a revelation that is revealed [to him], (4)
```

```arabic
عَلَّمَهُۥ شَدِيدُ ٱلْقُوَىٰ (٥)
```

```english
taught him by one of great powers, (5)
```

```arabic
ذُو مِرَّةٍ فَٱسْتَوَىٰ (٦)
```

```english
possessed of sound judgement. He settled, (6)
```

```arabic
وَهُوَ بِٱلْأُفُقِ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ (٧)
```

```english
while he was on the highest horizon. (7)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ دَنَا فَتَدَلَّىٰ (٨)
```

```english
Then he drew nearer and nearer (8)
```

```arabic
فَكَانَ قَابَ قَوْسَيْنِ أَوْ أَدْنَىٰ (٩)
```

```english
until he was within two bows’ length or even nearer, (9)
```

```arabic
فَأَوْحَىٰٓ إِلَىٰ عَبْدِهِۦ مَآ أَوْحَىٰ (١٠)
```

```english
whereat He revealed to His servant whatever He revealed. (10)
```

```arabic
مَا كَذَبَ ٱلْفُؤَادُ مَا رَأَىٰٓ (١١)
```

```english
The heart did not deny what it saw. (11)
```

```arabic
أَفَتُمَـٰرُونَهُۥ عَلَىٰ مَا يَرَىٰ (١٢)
```

```english
Will you then dispute with him about what he saw?! (12)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ رَءَاهُ نَزْلَةً أُخْرَىٰ (١٣)
```

```english
Certainly he saw it yet another time, (13)
```

```arabic
عِندَ سِدْرَةِ ٱلْمُنتَهَىٰ (١٤)
```

```english
by the Lote Tree of the Ultimate Boundary, (14)
```

```arabic
عِندَهَا جَنَّةُ ٱلْمَأْوَىٰٓ (١٥)
```

```english
near which is the Garden of the Abode, (15)
```

```arabic
إِذْ يَغْشَى ٱلسِّدْرَةَ مَا يَغْشَىٰ (١٦)
```

```english
when there covered the Lote Tree what covered it. (16)
```

```arabic
مَا زَاغَ ٱلْبَصَرُ وَمَا طَغَىٰ (١٧)
```

```english
His gaze did not swerve, nor did it overstep the bounds. (17)
```

```arabic
لَقَدْ رَأَىٰ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِ ٱلْكُبْرَىٰٓ (١٨)
```

```english
Certainly he saw some of the greatest signs of his Lord. (18)
```

## (Commentary)

The purpose of the surah is to remind of the three principles: His Oneness, the Exalted, in His Lordship, the Return (المعاد) — *the resurrection and final judgment*, and Prophethood (النبوة) — *the divine office of receiving and conveying God's message*. It begins with Prophethood, confirming the revelation (الوحي) — *the divine communication of truth* to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and describing it. Then it addresses Monotheism (الوحدانية) — *the absolute oneness of Allah*, negating idols and partners with the most emphatic negation. It then describes how the beginning of creation and its governance (التدبير) — *the divine management of all affairs* trace back to Him, the Exalted, including giving life, causing death, making one laugh or weep, enriching, providing, destroying, punishing, calling, and warning. It concludes the discourse by referring to the Return and the command for prostration and worship (العبادة) — *the ultimate submission and devotion to Allah*.

The surah is Meccan, as evidenced by the context of its verses, and no heed should be paid to the claim of some that some or all of its verses are Medinan. It has been said that it was the first surah the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله announced by reciting it; he recited it to the believers and the polytheists alike. Among the most brilliant verses within it are His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَنَّ إِلى رَبِّكَ الْمُنْتَهى** "and that the terminus is toward your Lord" (53:42), and His saying: **وَأَنْ لَيْسَ لِلْإِنْسانِ إِلَّا ما سَعى** "and that nothing belongs to man except what he strives for" (53:39).

The verses we have presented constitute the first of the three sections of the surah, which are the verses that confirm the revelation to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and describe it. However, there are numerous (مستفيضة) narrations from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه‌السلام explicitly stating that the intent of the verses is not to describe every revelation, but rather to describe the direct verbal revelation (وحي المشافهة) that Allah, the Glorified, revealed to His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله on the Night of the Ascension (المعراج) — *the miraculous night journey and ascent to the heavens*. Thus, the verses contain the story of the Ascension, and the apparent meaning of the verses is not without support for these narrations. This is also what is understood from the statements of some companions like Ibn Abbas, Anas, Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, and others, according to what is narrated from them. The commentators have followed this course, even though the disagreement among them is intense regarding the interpretation of its individual words and sentences.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَالنَّجْمِ إِذا هَوى** "By the star when it sets" (53:1). The apparent meaning of the verse is that the "star" (النجم) refers to any luminous celestial body (الجرم السماوي). Allah has sworn in His Book by many of His creations, including several celestial bodies like the sun, the moon, and the other planets. Accordingly, the "falling" (هوى) of the star refers to its setting for disappearance.

It has been said that the "star" refers to the Quran because of its gradual descent in installments (نجوماً). It has also been said it refers to the Pleiades (الثريا), or Sirius (الشعري), or the meteor (الشهاب) with which the devils of the jinn are pelted, because the Arabs call it a star. For each of these views, there is a meaning of "falling" (هوى) that suits it, but the wording of the verse does not support any of these specific meanings.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ما ضَلَّ صاحِبُكُمْ وَما غَوى** "your companion has neither gone astray, nor is he error-bound" (53:2). Straying (الضلال) is the departure and deviation from the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — *the direct and unerring way to Allah*. Error (الغي) is the opposite of right-guidance (الرشد), which is hitting upon reality. Al-Raghib said: "Error (الغي) is ignorance arising from a corrupt belief. This is because ignorance may stem from a person not holding any belief—neither sound nor corrupt—or it may stem from holding a corrupt belief. This second type is called *ghayy* (error). The Exalted said: **ما ضَلَّ صاحِبُكُمْ وَما غَوى**." End quote. The "companion" (الصاحب) refers to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

The meaning is: Your companion has not departed from the path leading to the desired goal, nor has he erred in his belief and opinion regarding it. The meaning returns to the fact that he did not err either in the desired goal—which is human felicity (السعادة الإنسانية) consisting of his servitude (عبودية) to the Exalted—nor in the path that leads to it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَما يَنْطِقُ عَنِ الْهَوى إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا وَحْيٌ يُوحى** "nor does he speak out of [personal] desire: it is just a revelation that is revealed [to him]" (53:3-4). What is meant by "desire" (الهوى) is the desire of the soul and its personal opinion. Although "speaking" (النطق) is mentioned generally and the negation is applied to it—which would imply the negation of desire from his speech صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله in its entirety—since it was an address to the polytheists who were accusing him in his call and what he recited to them of the Quran of being a liar, a fabricator, and an inventor against Allah, the context (قرينة المقام) indicates that he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله does not speak out of his own desire or opinion regarding what he calls you to of Allah or what he recites to you of the Quran; rather, it is nothing but a revelation revealed to him from Allah, the Glorified.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **عَلَّمَهُ شَدِيدُ الْقُوى** "taught him by one of great powers" (53:5). The pronoun in "taught him" (عَلَّمَهُ) refers to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, or to the Quran insofar as it is revelation, or to revelation in general. The second object of "taught" is omitted in any case; the estimation is: He taught the Prophet the revelation, or He taught the Quran, or the revelation [taught] him.

The one "of great powers" (شديد القوى)—according to what they have said—is Gabriel (جبريل). Allah described him with power in His saying: **ذِي قُوَّةٍ عِنْدَ ذِي الْعَرْشِ مَكِينٍ** "powerful and established in the presence of the Lord of the Throne" (81:20). It has also been said that it refers to Allah, the Glorified.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ذُو مِرَّةٍ فَاسْتَوى** "possessed of soundness. Then he settled" (53:6). *Mirrah* (المرة), with a *kasrah* on the *mim*, means strength (الشدة), soundness of intellect and opinion (حصافة العقل والرأي), or a form of passing (مرور). *Mirrah* in the verse has been interpreted by each of these three meanings while maintaining that the one "possessed of soundness" (ذو مرة) is Gabriel. The meaning would be: He—meaning Gabriel—is possessed of strength in the side of Allah, or he is possessed of soundness in his intellect and opinion, or he is possessed of a type of passing (مرور) by the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله while he was in the air.

It has also been said that the one "possessed of soundness" (ذو مرة) is the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله; thus he is possessed of strength in the side of Allah, or possessed of soundness in his intellect and opinion, or possessed of a type of passing (مرور) in which he ascended to the heavens.

His saying: **فَاسْتَوى** "Then he settled" means he stood straight or took control. The subject pronoun refers to Gabriel, and the meaning is: Gabriel stood straight in his original form in which he was created—according to what is narrated that Gabriel used to descend to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله in different forms, and he only appeared to him in his original form twice. Or the meaning is: Gabriel took control by his power over the matter assigned to him.

If the pronoun refers to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, the meaning is: he stood straight and became established.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَهُوَ بِالْأُفُقِ الْأَعْلى** "while he was on the highest horizon" (53:7). The "horizon" (الأفق) is the direction. It has been said that the "highest horizon" refers to the eastern direction of the sky because the eastern horizon is above the western one on the surface of the earth, not in the air—but this is as you see [weak]. It is apparent that what is meant is a horizon higher than the sky, without considering it an eastern horizon.

The pronoun "he" (هو) in the verse refers to Gabriel or to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and the sentence is a circumstantial clause (حال) for the pronoun in "he settled" (فَاسْتَوى).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ثُمَّ دَنا فَتَدَلَّى** "Then he drew near and hovered" (53:8). *Dunuww* (الدنو) is nearness, and *tadalli* (التدلي) is hanging onto something, used as a metaphor for extreme nearness. It has also been said it means extending toward the lower direction, derived from the bucket (الدلو).

The meaning—assuming both pronouns refer to Gabriel—is: Then Gabriel drew near and hung onto the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to ascend with him to the heavens. It has also been said: Then Gabriel hovered down from the highest horizon and drew near to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to ascend with him.

The meaning—assuming both pronouns refer to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله—is: Then the Prophet drew near to Allah, the Glorified, and increased in nearness.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَكانَ قابَ قَوْسَيْنِ أَوْ أَدْنى** "until he was within two bows’ length or even nearer" (53:9). It is said in *al-Majma'*: "*al-Qab* (القاب), *al-Qib* (القيب), *al-Qad* (القاد), and *al-Qayd* (القيد) are expressions for the measure of a thing." End quote. The "bow" (القوس) is well-known, the instrument for shooting. It is said that *qaws* also refers to the cubit (الذراع) in the dialect of the people of Hijaz, according to what has been said.

The meaning is: The distance was the measure of two bows, or the measure of two cubits, or nearer than that.

It has been said: *al-Qab* is the distance between the handle of the bow and its curved end (سيتها); thus there is an inversion (قلب) in the speech, and the meaning is: "he was the measure of two *qabs* of a bow." This was objected to on the grounds that "two *qabs* of a bow" and "the measure of two bows" are the same, so there is no reason for inversion.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَأَوْحى إِلى عَبْدِهِ ما أَوْحى** "whereupon He revealed to His servant whatever He revealed" (53:10). The pronoun in "revealed" (أوحى) in both instances refers to Gabriel, assuming the previous pronouns refer to Gabriel. The meaning would be: Gabriel revealed to the servant of Allah—who is the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله—whatever he revealed. It has been said: There is no harm in the pronoun referring to the Exalted despite the lack of a prior mention, because it is perfectly clear. Or, the three pronouns refer to Allah, and the meaning is: Allah revealed through the mediation of Gabriel to His servant whatever He revealed. Or, the first pronoun refers to Gabriel and the second and third to Allah, meaning: Gabriel revealed what Allah had revealed to him to the servant of Allah.

All three pronouns refer to Allah assuming the previous pronouns refer to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. The meaning is: Allah revealed to His servant whatever He revealed. This meaning is closer to the mind than the previous meaning, which sound taste (الذوق السليم) does not favor, even if it is correct.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ما كَذَبَ الْفُؤادُ ما رَأى** "The heart did not deny what it saw" (53:11). Falsehood (الكذب) is the opposite of truthfulness (الصدق). It is said: "So-and-so lied in his speech," and it is said: "He belied him the speech" (كذبه الحديث), making it transitive to two objects, meaning he told him a lie. Just as falsehood is applied to the word and speech uttered by the tongue, it is also applied to the error of the perceptive faculty (القوة المدركة). It is said: "His eye belied him," meaning it erred in its vision.

The negation of falsehood from the heart (الفؤاد) is in this sense, whether "lied" (كذب) is taken as intransitive—the estimation being: the heart did not lie in what it saw—or transitive to two objects—the estimation being: the heart—the heart of the Prophet—did not belie the Prophet in what he saw; meaning that the vision of his heart in what it saw was a truthful vision.

Accordingly, the "heart" (الفؤاد) refers to the heart of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and the subject pronoun in "what it saw" (ما رَأى) refers to the heart, and the vision is its vision.

There is no wonder in attributing vision—which is ocular witnessing (مشاهدة العيان)—to the heart. For man possesses a type of witness-based perception (إدراك شهودي) beyond perception by one of the external senses, imagination, or reflection by the internal faculties. Just as we witness from ourselves that we "see," and this ocular witnessing is neither sight with the eye nor something known through thought. Likewise, we see from ourselves that we hear, smell, taste, and touch, and we witness that we imagine and reflect. This "seeing" is not with the eye or any of the external or internal senses. For just as we witness the percepts of each of these faculties through that very faculty, we also witness our own perception of its percept. This witnessing is not through that faculty itself, but through ourselves, expressed as the "heart" (الفؤاد).

There is nothing in the verse indicating that the object of vision is Allah, the Glorified, and that He was seen by him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. Rather, the seen objects are the highest horizon, the drawing near, the hovering, and that He revealed to him; these are what are mentioned in the previous verses, and they are signs (آيات) of Him, the Exalted. This is supported by what He, the Exalted, mentioned regarding the other descent in His saying: **ما زاغَ الْبَصَرُ وَما طَغى لَقَدْ رَأى مِنْ آياتِ رَبِّهِ الْكُبْرى** "The eye did not swerve, nor did it overstep the bounds. Certainly he saw some of the greatest signs of his Lord" (53:17-18).

Furthermore, even if it did indicate that the vision pertained to Him, the Exalted, there would be no harm in it, for it would be the vision of the heart. The vision of the heart is different from the sensory vision of the eye which pertains to bodies, and it is impossible for it to pertain to Him, the Exalted. We have previously presented a discourse on the vision of the heart in the commentary (تفسير) of Surah al-A'raf, verse 143.

As for what has been said: that the pronoun in "what it saw" (ما رَأى) refers to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and the meaning is: his heart صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله did not say to what he saw with his eye, "I do not recognize you," for if it had said that, it would have been lying, because he recognized it with his heart just as he saw it with his eye—the sum of which is that his heart confirmed his sight in what it saw.

Likewise, what has been said: that the meaning is that his heart did not belie his sight in what it saw, but rather confirmed it and believed in it. This is supported by the reading of those who read **ما كَذَّبَ** with a *shaddah* on the *dhal*.

The response to this is that what the context of the verses provides is His, the Exalted, support for the truthfulness of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله in what he claims of revelation and the vision of the greatest signs of Allah. If the pronoun in "what it saw" (ما رَأى) referred to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, the sum of the verse's meaning would be an argument for the truthfulness of his vision based on his heart's belief in it, which is far from the style of the Quran. This is unlike if the pronoun in "what it saw" (ما رَأى) returns to the heart, for the sum of its meaning is His, the Exalted, confirmation of his heart in what it saw, and the discourse follows the previous context starting from His saying: **ما ضَلَّ صاحِبُكُمْ وَما غَوى إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا وَحْيٌ يُوحى** etc.

**If you say:** He, the Exalted, argues in the following verse **أَفَتُمارُونَهُ عَلى ما يَرى** "Will you then dispute with him about what he saw?" (53:12) by his vision صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله for his truthfulness in what he claims, so let the argument by his heart's belief in what he sees with his eye be similar to it.

**I say:** His saying: **أَفَتُمارُونَهُ عَلى ما يَرى** is not intended to argue by his vision for his truthfulness, but rather as a rebuke for their disputing with him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله over a matter he sees and observes ocularly. Disputation (المماراة) and argumentation (المجادلة) are only valid—if they are valid at all—regarding theoretical opinions and intellectual beliefs. As for what is seen and witnessed ocularly, there is no meaning for disputation and argumentation regarding it. He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was only informing them of what he witnessed ocularly, not from thought or intellection.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَفَتُمارُونَهُ عَلى ما يَرى** "Will you then dispute with him about what he saw?" (53:12). The interrogative is for rebuke, the address is to the polytheists, and the pronoun refers to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. Disputation (المماراة) is persistence in argumentation. The meaning is: Do you persist in your argument against the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله that he should submit to the opposite of what he claims and informs you of, while he witnesses that ocularly?

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَقَدْ رَآهُ نَزْلَةً أُخْرى** "He certainly saw him yet another time" (53:13). *Nazlah* (النزلة) is the noun of instance (بناء مرة) from "descending" (النزول), so it means one descent. The verse indicates that this is the story of a vision in another descent, and the previous verses narrate another descent besides it.

They have said: The latent subject pronoun in His saying **رَآهُ** refers to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and the object pronoun refers to Gabriel. Accordingly, the "descent" (النزلة) is the descent of Gabriel upon him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to ascend with him to the heavens. His saying: **عِنْدَ سِدْرَةِ الْمُنْتَهى** "by the Lote Tree of the Ultimate Boundary" (53:14) is an adverb of place for the vision, not for the descent. What is meant by seeing him is seeing him while he was in his original form.

The meaning is: He descended upon him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله another descent and ascended with him to the heavens, and he appeared to him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله at the Lote Tree of the Ultimate Boundary while he was in his original form.

It has become clear from what preceded the correctness of referring the object pronoun to Him, the Exalted. What is meant by vision is the vision of the heart, and what is meant by "another descent" is the descent of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله at the Lote Tree of the Ultimate Boundary during his ascent to the heavens. Thus, the meaning is that he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله descended another descent during his ascension at the Lote Tree of the Ultimate Boundary and saw Him with his heart just as he saw Him in the first descent.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **عِنْدَ سِدْرَةِ الْمُنْتَهى عِنْدَها جَنَّةُ الْمَأْوى إِذْ يَغْشَى السِّدْرَةَ ما يَغْشى** "by the Lote Tree of the Ultimate Boundary, near which is the Garden of the Abode, when there covered the Lote Tree what covered it" (53:14-16). *Sidr* (السدر) is a well-known tree, and the *ta* is for the singular. The "Ultimate Boundary" (المنتهى)—it seems—is a noun of place. Perhaps what is meant by it is the boundary of the heavens, evidenced by the Garden being near it, and the Garden is in the heaven. The Exalted said: **وَفِي السَّماءِ رِزْقُكُمْ وَما تُوعَدُونَ** "And in the heaven is your provision and what you are promised" (51:22).

There is nothing in His speech, the Exalted, that explains this tree, and the intent was to leave it vague, as supported by His saying afterward: **إِذْ يَغْشَى السِّدْرَةَ ما يَغْشى**. It has also been interpreted in the narrations as a tree above the seventh heaven, to which the deeds of the children of Adam reach; some of these narrations will follow.

His saying: **عِنْدَها جَنَّةُ الْمَأْوى** "near which is the Garden of the Abode" (53:15), meaning the Garden to which the believers take refuge, and it is the Garden of the Hereafter. For the Garden of the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — *the state between death and resurrection* is a hastened garden limited by the Resurrection. The Exalted said: **فَلَهُمْ جَنَّاتُ الْمَأْوى نُزُلاً بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** "for them will be the gardens of the Abode as a hospitality for what they used to do" (32:19), and His saying: **فَإِذا جاءَتِ الطَّامَّةُ الْكُبْرى**—until He said—**فَإِنَّ الْجَنَّةَ هِيَ الْمَأْوى** "then the Garden will indeed be the abode" (79:41). It is in the heaven according to what is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **وَفِي السَّماءِ رِزْقُكُمْ وَما تُوعَدُونَ** (51:22). It has also been said: What is meant by it is the Garden of the intermediate realm.

His saying: **إِذْ يَغْشَى السِّدْرَةَ ما يَغْشى** "when there covered the Lote Tree what covered it" (53:16). The "covering" (غشيان) of a thing is to encompass it. The "what" (ما) is a relative pronoun, and the meaning is: when there encompassed the Lote Tree what encompassed it. Allah, the Exalted, has left vague that which covered the Lote Tree and did not clarify what it was, as previously indicated.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ما زاغَ الْبَصَرُ وَما طَغى** "The eye did not swerve, nor did it overstep the bounds" (53:17). *Zaygh* (الزيغ) is the deviation from uprightness. *Tughyan* (الطغيان) is exceeding the limit in action. The swerving of the eye is its perception of the object of sight differently from how it actually is, and its overstepping is its perception of that which has no reality. What is meant by the "eye" (البصر) is the eye of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله.

The meaning is: He صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله did not see what he saw in other than its true attribute, nor did he see what has no reality; rather, he saw without being mistaken in his seeing.

What is meant by "seeing" (الإبصار) is his vision صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله with his heart, not with the physical organ of the eye. For what is meant by this seeing is what He means by His saying: **وَلَقَد| رَآهُ نَزْلَةً أُخْرى**, which points to the similarity of this vision to the vision of the first descent, to which He points by His saying: **ما كَذَبَ الْفُؤادُ ما رَأى أَفَتُمارُونَهُ عَلى ما يَرى**. So understand and do not be heedless.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَقَد| رَأى مِنْ آياتِ رَبِّهِ الْكُبْرى** "Certainly he saw some of the greatest signs of his Lord" (53:18). The "from" (مِنْ) is for partitivity (التبعيض). The meaning is: I swear, he certainly witnessed some of the greatest signs of his Lord. By that, the witnessing of his Lord with his heart was completed. For the witnessing of Him, the Exalted, with the heart is only through the witnessing of His signs insofar as they are His signs. For the sign (الآية), insofar as it is a sign, only tells of the possessor of the sign and does not tell anything of itself; otherwise, it would not be a sign from that aspect.

As for witnessing His transcendent Essence (الذات) without the mediation of a sign or the intervention of a veil, that is impossible. The Exalted said: **وَلا يُحِيطُونَ بِهِ عِلْماً** "and they do not comprehend Him in knowledge" (20:110).

## (Narrative Discussion)

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَالنَّجْمِ إِذا هَوى**, he said: "The star is the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله; **إِذا هَوى** (when it sets) refers to when he was taken on the Night Journey to the heaven and he was in the descent (الهوي)."

**I say:** His being named "the star" صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله has been narrated with his chain of transmission from his father, from al-Husayn ibn Khalid, from Imam al-Rida عليه‌السلام, and it is from the inner meaning (البطن).

In *al-Kafi*, from al-Qummi, from his father, from Ibn Abi 'Umayr, from Muhammad ibn Muslim, who said: I said to Abu Ja'far عليه‌السلام: "The saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَاللَّيْلِ إِذا يَغْشى** (By the night when it covers), **وَالنَّجْمِ إِذا هَوى** (By the star when it sets), and what is similar to that?" He said: "**Indeed, Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, may swear by whatever of His creation He wills, but His creation may not swear except by Him.**"

**I say:** In *al-Faqih*, from Ali ibn Mahziyar, from Abu Ja'far the Second عليه‌السلام: the same.

In *al-Majma'*: The general public (العامة) narrated from Ja'far al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام that he said: "**Indeed, Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله descended from the seventh heaven on the Night of the Ascension. When the surah was revealed, 'Utbah ibn Abi Lahab was informed of that, so he came to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, divorced his daughter, spat in his face, and said: 'I disbelieve in the star and the Lord of the star.' So he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله prayed against him and said: 'O Allah, set upon him one of Your dogs.'**"

'Utbah set out for Syria and stopped at a place on the way—and Allah cast terror into him. He said to his companions: "Make me sleep in your midst tonight." They did so, and a lion came and snatched him from among the people.

**I say:** Then al-Tabarsi cited the poetry of Hassan regarding that. It is narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* with various chains.

In *al-Kafi*, with his chain to Hisham, Hammad, and others, they said: We heard Abu 'Abdillah عليه‌السلام saying: "**My narration is the narration of my father, and the narration of my father is the narration of my grandfather, and the narration of my grandfather is the narration of al-Husayn, and the narration of al-Husayn is the narration of al-Hasan, and the narration of al-Hasan is the narration of the Commander of the Faithful, and the narration of the Commander of the Faithful is the narration of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and the narration of the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic.**"

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, with his chain to Ibn Sinan in a narration: Abu 'Abdillah عليه‌السلام said: "**And that is because he**"—meaning the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله—"**is the closest of creation to Allah, the Exalted. He was at the place where Gabriel said to him when he was taken on the Night Journey to the heaven: 'Step forward, O Muhammad, for you have trodden a place where no archangel nor any sent prophet has ever trodden.' Had his spirit and soul not been from that place, he would not have been able to reach it. He was from Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, as Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, said: **قابَ قَوْسَيْنِ أَوْ أَدْنى** (within two bows’ length or even nearer), meaning: rather, nearer.**"

In *al-Ihtijaj*, from Ali ibn al-Husayn عليه‌السلام in a long narration: "**I am the son of him who ascended and became exalted, passing the Lote Tree of the Ultimate Boundary—until he was from his Lord within two bows’ length or even nearer.**"

**I say:** This meaning has been mentioned in many narrations of the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt عليه‌السلام.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn al-Mundhir and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Sa'id al-Khudri, who said: "When the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was taken on the Night Journey, he drew near to his Lord—until he was within two bows’ length or even nearer." He said: "Have you not seen the bow, how close it is to the string?"

In it, Ibn Abi Hatim, al-Tabarani, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abbas: regarding His saying: **ثُمَّ دَنا فَتَدَلَّى**, he said: "It is Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله; he drew near and hovered toward his Lord, the Mighty and Majestic."

In *al-Majma'*, it is narrated as a *marfu'* narration from Anas, who said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: regarding His saying: **فَكانَ قابَ قَوْسَيْنِ أَوْ أَدْنى**, he said: "**The measure of two cubits or nearer than two cubits.**"

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **فَأَوْحى إِلى عَبْدِهِ ما أَوْحى**, he said: "**A direct verbal revelation.**"

In *al-Tawhid*, with his chain to Muhammad ibn al-Fudayl, who said: I asked Abu al-Hasan عليه‌السلام: "Did the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله see his Lord, the Mighty and Majestic?" He said: "**Yes, he saw Him with his heart. Have you not heard Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, saying: **ما كَذَبَ الْفُؤادُ ما رَأى** (The heart did not deny what it saw)? He did not see Him with the sight, but he saw Him with the heart.**"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, 'Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn al-Mundhir, and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Muhammad ibn Ka'b al-Qurazi from some of the companions of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, who said: They said: "O Messenger of Allah, have you seen your Lord?" He said: "**I did not see Him with my eye, but I saw Him with my heart twice.**" Then he recited: **ثُمَّ دَنا فَتَدَلَّى**.

**I say:** Al-Nasa'i narrated this meaning from Abu Dharr as mentioned in *al-Durr al-Manthur*, and its wording is: "**The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله saw his Lord with his heart and did not see Him with his sight.**"

From *Sahih Muslim*, al-Tirmidhi, and Ibn Mardawayh from Abu Dharr, who said: I asked the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "Have you seen your Lord?" He said: "**He is Luminous (نوراني); I see Him.**"

**I say:** "Luminous" (نوراني) is attributed to light (النور) contrary to the standard rule, like "corporeal" (جسماني) in attribution to body (جسم). It has also been read as "A light that I see" (نورٌ إني أراه) with the nunnation of the *ra*, the *kasrah* of the *hamzah*, and the *shaddah* of the *nun* followed by the first-person pronoun. It appears that this is a scribal error, even if supported by another narration from Muslim in his *Sahih* and Ibn Mardawayh from Abu Dharr: that he asked the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "Have you seen your Lord?" He said: "**I saw a light.**"

In any case, what is meant by vision is the vision of the heart; thus, the vision is not sensory, nor is the light sensory.

In *al-Kafi*, with his chain from Safwan ibn Yahya, who said: Abu Qurrah the traditionist asked me to take him to Abu al-Hasan al-Rida عليه‌السلام. I asked his permission for that, and he permitted me. He entered upon him and asked him about the lawful, the prohibited, and the rulings... until he said: Abu Qurrah said: "For He says: **وَلَقَدْ رَآهُ نَزْلَةً أُخْرى** (He certainly saw him yet another time)." Abu al-Hasan عليه‌السلام said: "**Indeed, after this verse is that which indicates what he saw, where He said: **ما كَذَبَ الْفُؤادُ ما رَأى** (The heart did not deny what it saw). He says: The heart of Muhammad did not deny what his eyes saw. Then He informed of what he saw, saying: **لَقَدْ رَأى مِنْ آياتِ رَبِّهِ الْكُبْرى** (Certainly he saw some of the greatest signs of his Lord). And the signs of Allah are other than Allah.**"

**I say:** It is apparent that his speech عليه‌السلام was intended to silence Abu Qurrah, as he wanted to prove His vision, the Exalted, with the sensory eye. So he compelled him with the fact that the vision only pertained to the signs, and the signs of Allah are other than Allah. This does not contradict the fact that the vision of the signs insofar as they are His signs is a vision of Him, even if His signs are other than Him. This vision was only with the heart, as several narrations have passed regarding this meaning.

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, my father narrated to me from Ibn Abi 'Umayr, from Hisham, from Abu 'Abdillah عليه‌السلام, who said: The Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "**I reached the Lote Tree of the Ultimate Boundary, and behold, a single leaf of it shades a nation among the nations. I was from my Lord as within two bows’ length or even nearer.**"

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ahmad and Ibn Jarir narrated from Anas, who said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "**I reached the Lote Tree, and behold, its fruit was like jars, and its leaves were like the ears of elephants. When there covered it of the command of Allah what covered it, it turned into rubies, emeralds, and the like.**"

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, with his chain to Isma'il al-Ju'fi, from Abu Ja'far عليه‌السلام in a long narration: "**When he reached the Lote Tree of the Ultimate Boundary, Gabriel stayed behind. The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'In this place you desert me?' He said: 'Step forward before you, for by Allah, you have reached a place that no one of Allah's creation before you has reached.' So I saw of the light of my Lord, and the *Subhah* (السبحة) intervened between me and Him.**"

I said: "What is the *Subhah*, may I be your ransom?" He gestured with his face toward the earth and gestured with his hand toward the heaven while saying: "**The Majesty of my Lord, the Majesty of my Lord**"—three times.

**I say:** The *Subhah* is the Majesty (الجلال), as interpreted in the narration. The *Subhah* is that which indicates His transcendence, the Exalted, from His creation, and its return is to the first meaning. The sum of the end of the narration is that he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله saw his Lord by seeing His signs.

In it: regarding His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ رَآهُ نَزْلَةً أُخْرى عِنْدَ سِدْرَةِ الْمُنْتَهى**, he said: "**In the seventh heaven.**"

**In it:** regarding His saying, the Exalted: **إِذْ يَغْشَى السِّدْرَةَ ما يَغْشى**, he said: "**When the veil was lifted between Him and the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله—the light of the Lote Tree covered [everything].**"

**I say:** There are other narrations regarding the previous meanings. Comprehensive narrations regarding the story of his Ascension صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله have already preceded at the beginning of the commentary of Surah al-Isra'.

We cited there, following the narrations, the disagreement regarding the nature of his Ascension صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: whether it was in a dream or while awake; and if the latter, with his body and soul together or with his soul only. We cited from the author of *al-Manaqib* that the Imamiyyah hold that his Night Journey from the Sacred Mosque to the al-Aqsa Mosque was with both soul and body together, as indicated by the verse of the Night Journey. As for from the al-Aqsa Mosque to the heavens, a group said it was also with both soul and body together, and many of the Shi'a agreed with them, while some inclined toward it being with the soul, and some later scholars inclined toward this.

There is no harm in holding this view if it is supported by the context surrounding the verses and narrations. However, it would then be necessary to interpret His saying, the Exalted: **عِنْدَها جَنَّةُ الْمَأْوى** as the Garden of the intermediate realm, so that its being "near it" is interpreted as a type of connection, as it is narrated that the grave is either a garden from the gardens of Paradise or a pit from the pits of Hell; or to direct the verse in a way that does not contradict the ascension in the heavens being spiritual.

As for the Night Journey being in a dream, it has already been mentioned in the commentary of the verse of the Night Journey that it is something that should not be paid attention to.

As for applying the Ascension to the heavens to his traveling صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله at night to other planets besides the Earth in our solar system, or in other systems besides ours, or in other galaxies besides our galaxy—this is something that the reports narrated in the details of the story do not suit at all, nor do the contents of the preceding verses.
